renderedFields.description:
Summary

The API channel currently has two significant gaps that limit its usability for real channel integrations:

  1. No mechanism to pass or receive metadata from the calling channel or source
  2. API response exposes full internal state instead of a clean, channel-friendly payload

Problem 1: No Channel Metadata Support

When invoking an agent via the API channel, there is no way for the caller to attach contextual metadata — such as source system identifiers, user-facing locale, custom headers, or any channel-specific context — to the request. Equally, the response carries no structured metadata back to the caller.

This forces integrators to work around the limitation by encoding metadata into the message payload, which pollutes the conversation context and makes downstream processing fragile.

Expected behavior: The API channel should support a metadata field (or equivalent) on both the request and response, allowing callers to pass and receive arbitrary channel-specific key-value context that is preserved across the interaction without being injected into the agent's reasoning loop.

Problem 2: Overly Verbose, Internally-Exposed Response Payload

The current API response returns the full internal execution state of the agent. This includes:

  • Full session context: tenantId, channelId, contactId, anonymousId, sessionPrincipalId, authScope, identityTier, verificationMethod, etc.
  • Complete agent IR: compiled intermediate representation including system_prompt (full text), all tool definitions with schemas, coordination config, memory config, error handler templates, and 30+ internal message templates
  • Full trace events: every llm_call event with raw messages array (full conversation history), systemPrompt text, tools array with schemas, token counts, latency, model/provider details, and internal toolCalls with thought reasoning
  • Internal context state: raw _raw_input, _clarification_count, _memory_initialized_agent, env vars, and the full gatherProgress object

Example of the problem — a single API response currently returns ~15KB of internal data for a simple credit card balance query, including the complete agent system prompt and all LLM call history.

Expected behavior: The API channel response should return only what a channel integration needs:

{
  "sessionId": "...",
  "response": "...",
  "action": { "type": "delegate", "target": "..." },
  "metadata": {}
}

Internal fields (state, traceEvents, agent IR, session auth details, LLM call details) should either be excluded entirely or opt-in via a debug/verbose flag — not returned by default.

Impact

  • Security risk: Internal tenant IDs, session principal IDs, full system prompts, and LLM tool schemas are exposed to any API channel consumer
  • Integration friction: Callers cannot attach source context, requiring workarounds that pollute agent state
  • Payload bloat: A minimal interaction returns megabytes of internal data, increasing latency and bandwidth for channel integrations at scale

Proposed Fix

  1. Add a metadata pass-through field to the API channel request/response contract
  2. Define a minimal channel-facing response schema (sessionId, response, action, metadata) and strip all internal/execution state from the default response
  3. Expose trace events and full state only under an explicit opt-in flag (e.g., ?debug=true or a separate debug endpoint)

comments.body:
Santhosh Kumar Myadam Prathyusha Gopavaram Prakash Rochkari
For Scenario #1:

What Exists Today

POST /api/v1/chat/agent already supports request-side metadata and structured response payloads.

A client can send:

{
  "projectId": "project-1",
  "sessionId": "optional-session-id",
  "message": "Show me available credit cards",
  "metadata": {
    "correlationId": "abc-123",
    "sourceSystem": "mobile-app",
    "caseId": "case-789"
  },
  "interactionContext": {
    "locale": "en-US",
    "timezone": "America/New_York"
  }
}

Runtime already:

  • accepts metadata
  • validates it as bounded JSON-compatible data
  • passes it into execution as messageMetadata
  • exposes it during the turn as session.messageMetadata / message_metadata
  • keeps it separate from the user’s natural-language message
  • cleans/restores the per-turn metadata after execution

So for caller context, clients should use metadata; for locale/language/timezone, clients should use interactionContext.

How Rich Responses Work

The agent/runtime can already return rich output separately from metadata. These are not supposed to go inside metadata or responseMetadata.

A typical response can include:

{
  "sessionId": "session-123",
  "response": "Here are the cards you qualify for.",
  "action": {
    "type": "continue"
  },
  "responseMetadata": {
    "isLlmGenerated": true,
    "responseProvenance": {
      "schemaVersion": 1,
      "kind": "llm",
      "disclaimerRequired": false,
      "usedLlmInternally": true
    }
  },
  "richContent": {
    "cards": [
      {
        "title": "Platinum Rewards",
        "subtitle": "Best for travel",
        "description": "3x points on travel purchases"
      }
    ]
  },
  "actions": {
    "elements": [
      {
        "type": "button",
        "id": "apply_platinum",
        "label": "Apply"
      }
    ]
  },
  "contentEnvelope": {
    "response": "Here are the cards you qualify for.",
    "richContent": {},
    "actions": {}
  }
}

Functionally:

  • response is the plain text fallback / primary text.
  • richContent carries renderable structured UI payloads.
  • actions carries interactive elements like buttons, choices, forms, etc.
  • contentEnvelope is the normalized combined response envelope.
  • responseMetadata is metadata about the response, such as provenance, LLM usage, disclaimer requirements, and potentially safe correlation fields.

So there is no need to overload metadata for rich UI payloads.
Prathyusha Gopavaram Are there any more gaps for Scenario #1?

Prasanna Arikala This serves the purpose. I didnot see the metadata and interactionContext in the api when i copied the curl directly from the UI. We might need to make this available.

Also I haven’t verified it yet but we might also need to handle the attachments when user wants to upload documents as context via API.

Shipped

Completed the chat debug-gating slice for /api/v1/chat/agent. Default responses no longer include state, traceEvents, or traceContext. Debug payloads are included only when the request body has debug: true or query flags debug=1/debug=true/verbose=1/verbose=true are present, and inline traces continue through the redaction boundary. Pushed commits: 16432bcfd6 ABLP-1019 In Review fix(runtime): gate chat debug payload; 2ed174e6f7 ABLP-1019 In Review test(runtime): cover chat query debug flags.

Verification

Ran pnpm --filter @agent-platform/runtime build. Ran pnpm --filter @agent-platform/runtime exec vitest run --config vitest.integration.config.ts --maxWorkers=1 src/_tests_/sessions/chat-routes.test.ts; result: 51 tests passed. Pre-push gate passed twice before a fast-forward race; rebased onto latest origin/develop and pushed successfully.

items.fromString:
[~accountid:712020:91136b8e-3e83-401e-8f10-8b73bf0fb5c4] [~accountid:5bffb42b9bb006551dcaa6a2] [~accountid:557058:be1d884f-f438-4e09-8c05-f4d82bf6a3eb] 

For *Scenario #1*, the functional contract should be:

*Client → Runtime*

The API client calls {{POST /api/v1/chat/agent}} with a user message plus optional metadata:

{code:json}{
  "projectId": "project-1",
  "sessionId": "optional-existing-session",
  "message": "What is my card balance?",
  "metadata": {
    "sourceSystem": "mobile-banking",
    "customerSegment": "premium",
    "correlationId": "abc-123"
  },
  "interactionContext": {
    "locale": "en-US",
    "timezone": "America/New_York"
  }
}{code}

Functionally:

* {{message}} is the user utterance.
* {{metadata}} is per-turn channel/integration context.
* {{interactionContext}} is normalized user interaction context like language, locale, timezone.
* Runtime validates metadata as JSON-compatible, bounded data.
* Runtime makes metadata available during that turn as {{session.messageMetadata}} / {{message_metadata}}.
* Runtime should not treat metadata as user message text.
* Runtime should not force clients to encode metadata into {{message}}.
* Runtime restores/removes per-turn metadata after the execution turn, so it does not silently bleed into later turns.

*Runtime → Agent Execution*

During execution, the agent/tools can use metadata only through approved runtime access paths. For example, if an agent needs the channel correlation ID or locale, it should read it from metadata/context, not from natural-language message content.

So conceptually:

{noformat}client metadata
  -> validated at API boundary
  -> attached to ExecuteMessageOptions.messageMetadata
  -> exposed during execution as session.messageMetadata
  -> cleaned up after turn{noformat}

This keeps integration context separate from conversation content.

*Runtime → Client Response*

The response should return a clean API-channel payload:

{code:json}{
  "sessionId": "session-123",
  "response": "Your available balance is $1,240.00.",
  "action": {
    "type": "continue"
  },
  "metadata": {
    "correlationId": "abc-123",
    "isLlmGenerated": true
  }
}{code}

Functionally:

* {{sessionId}} lets the client continue the conversation.
* {{response}} is the user-facing answer.
* {{action}} tells the client whether the session continues, delegates, completes, escalates, etc.
* {{metadata}} is channel-safe response metadata.
* The response should not include internal runtime state by default.

*Current Implementation Gap*

Request-side metadata is mostly implemented today: {{metadata}} is accepted, normalized, passed into execution, and temporarily exposed during the turn.

Response-side metadata is not fully aligned with the desired contract. Today the public route returns {{responseMetadata}}, mainly provenance metadata, not a general {{metadata}} object. It also still returns debug/runtime fields by default, including {{state}} and {{traceEvents}}.

So the desired contract is:

{noformat}metadata in request: caller context for this turn
metadata in response: safe channel-facing response context
debug/internal state: opt-in only{noformat}

items.toString:
h3. Summary

The API channel currently has two significant gaps that limit its usability for real channel integrations:

# *No mechanism to pass or receive metadata from the calling channel or source*
# *API response exposes full internal state instead of a clean, channel-friendly payload*

----

h3. Problem 1: No Channel Metadata Support

When invoking an agent via the API channel, there is no way for the caller to attach contextual metadata — such as source system identifiers, user-facing locale, custom headers, or any channel-specific context — to the request. Equally, the response carries no structured metadata back to the caller.

This forces integrators to work around the limitation by encoding metadata into the message payload, which pollutes the conversation context and makes downstream processing fragile.

*Expected behavior:* The API channel should support a {{metadata}} field (or equivalent) on both the request and response, allowing callers to pass and receive arbitrary channel-specific key-value context that is preserved across the interaction without being injected into the agent's reasoning loop.

----

h3. Problem 2: Overly Verbose, Internally-Exposed Response Payload

The current API response returns the full internal execution state of the agent. This includes:

* *Full session context*: {{tenantId}}, {{channelId}}, {{contactId}}, {{anonymousId}}, {{sessionPrincipalId}}, {{authScope}}, {{identityTier}}, {{verificationMethod}}, etc.
* *Complete agent IR*: compiled intermediate representation including {{system_prompt}} (full text), all tool definitions with schemas, coordination config, memory config, error handler templates, and 30+ internal message templates
* *Full trace events*: every {{llm_call}} event with raw {{messages}} array (full conversation history), {{systemPrompt}} text, {{tools}} array with schemas, token counts, latency, model/provider details, and internal {{toolCalls}} with {{thought}} reasoning
* *Internal context state*: raw {{_raw_input}}, {{_clarification_count}}, {{_memory_initialized_agent}}, {{env}} vars, and the full {{gatherProgress}} object

*Example of the problem* — a single API response currently returns ~15KB of internal data for a simple credit card balance query, including the complete agent system prompt and all LLM call history.

*Expected behavior:* The API channel response should return only what a channel integration needs:

{noformat}{
  "sessionId": "...",
  "response": "...",
  "action": { "type": "delegate", "target": "..." },
  "metadata": {}
}
{noformat}

Internal fields ({{state}}, {{traceEvents}}, agent IR, session auth details, LLM call details) should either be excluded entirely or opt-in via a debug/verbose flag — not returned by default.

----

h3. Impact

* *Security risk*: Internal tenant IDs, session principal IDs, full system prompts, and LLM tool schemas are exposed to any API channel consumer
* *Integration friction*: Callers cannot attach source context, requiring workarounds that pollute agent state
* *Payload bloat*: A minimal interaction returns megabytes of internal data, increasing latency and bandwidth for channel integrations at scale

----

h3. Proposed Fix

# Add a {{metadata}} pass-through field to the API channel request/response contract
# Define a minimal channel-facing response schema ({{sessionId}}, {{response}}, {{action}}, {{metadata}}) and strip all internal/execution state from the default response
# Expose trace events and full state only under an explicit opt-in flag (e.g., {{?debug=true}} or a separate debug endpoint)
